votes.reason: The Python code correctly implements the logical conditions of the Coq specification, including the existential check for k >= 0 by iterating and breaking when the absolute value exceeds |x|.
The Python code correctly implements the logical conditions specified in the Coq definition, including handling edge cases for n=0, 1, -1 and correctly searching for k >= 0 when |n| > 1.

The Python code correctly implements the logical conditions of the Coq specification, handling the explicit disjunctions and correctly searching for a valid exponent k when |n| > 1 by leveraging the monotonic growth of |n^k|.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. It checks all the disjunctive conditions (x=1, n=0 and x=0, n=1 and x=1, n=-1 and x in {1,-1}) and then searches for k>=0 such that n^k=x. The condition abs(n^k)<=abs(x) is automatically satisfied when n^k=x. The Python code returns whether b matches the computed condition, which corresponds to the Coq biconditional b=true <-> condition. For all concrete inputs, the Python code will return the same judgment as the Coq specification.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. It checks whether b equals the condition that x is a power of n (with the special cases handled). The condition in Coq is a biconditional (b = true <-> condition), which means b should be true iff the condition holds. The Python code computes whether the condition holds and returns whether b matches that computed condition. All the special cases (x=1, n=0 and x=0, n=1 and x=1, n=-1 with x=1 or x=-1) and the general existential case (finding k>=0 such that n^k=x) are correctly handled. The redundancy in checking some cases twice doesn't affect correctness.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. It checks if b equals the condition that x is a power of n (with the special cases handled). The condition in Coq is a biconditional (b = true <-> condition), which means b should be true iff the condition holds. The Python code computes whether the condition holds and returns whether b matches that computed condition. All the disjunctive cases (x=1, n=0 and x=0, n=1 and x=1, n=-1 and x in {1,-1}, or exists k>=0 with n^k=x) are properly checked. The existential search terminates because for |n|>1, powers grow unboundedly, and special cases for n in {-1,0,1} are handled separately.

Implements the same disjunctions (x=1; n in {0,1,-1} special cases) and checks existence of k>=0 with n**k=x for other n, with Python ints matching Coq Z and 0**0=1. The abs inequality is redundant when n**k=x. Returns b==condition as required.
It checks the same disjunctions and searches for k >= 0 with n**k = x, handling 0, 1, and -1 consistently with Coq Z.pow (including 0**0 = 1). Returns b == condition, matching b = true <-> ...

Python mirrors the Coq disjunctions and searches k >= 0 for n^k = x (with correct special cases for n in {0,1,-1} and 0^0 = 1). It returns b == condition, matching b = true <-> condition for all inputs.
